content.text: FastAPI is a modern Python web framework for building HTTP APIs. It is built on top of Starlette (the ASGI transport layer) and Pydantic (the data validation library). FastAPI routes are async by default, meaning the framework leverages Python's asyncio event loop to handle many concurrent requests without blocking. All request bodies, path parameters, and query parameters are declared as Python type annotations, and FastAPI validates them automatically using Pydantic.

FastAPI was created by Sebastián Ramírez in 2018 and has grown to become one of the most popular Python frameworks for new API projects. Its key insight was to take Python's type hints — already familiar to developers using mypy or IDEs — and make them do double duty: validating incoming data and generating OpenAPI documentation simultaneously. This eliminates entire categories of boilerplate that other frameworks require.

The framework is used in production by large organisations running AI inference APIs, data pipelines, and microservices. Its native async support makes it well suited to the IO-heavy workloads common in agentic systems: calling LLM APIs, querying databases, fetching external data, and streaming results back to clients.

Python has historically been a synchronous language for web development. Frameworks like Flask and Django use a blocking, thread-per-request model. Under light load this is fine; under heavy concurrent load (streaming LLM responses, long-polling, tool-call pipelines), threads become the bottleneck. FastAPI's async-first design maps directly onto the event-driven IO patterns that AI services require.

For agent developers, FastAPI also reduces the distance from "idea" to "working API". Defining a Pydantic model gives you free validation, free serialisation, and free OpenAPI documentation. There is no separate step to write API documentation or write validators — the type annotations are the documentation and the validators.

FastAPI is consistently benchmarked as one of the fastest Python web frameworks — comparable to Node.js Express in throughput on IO-bound tasks — because it runs on uvicorn (an ASGI server built on uvloop) and avoids per-request thread overhead.
every parameter is declared with Python types and validated automatically. Pydantic catches malformed input before it reaches business logic.
and a fully interactive API spec is generated from your type annotations — no manual YAML.
run in the asyncio event loop. Multiple in-flight requests share one thread, dramatically improving throughput for IO-bound workloads.
system declaratively injects database sessions, authenticated users, configuration, or any callable result into handlers.
and async generator support make it easy to stream LLM tokens, file downloads, or server-sent events.
Pydantic v2 (FastAPI's current validator) is written in Rust and validates at 10–50x the speed of earlier Python validators, keeping validation overhead below one millisecond for typical payloads.
mixing sync and async is easy to get wrong. Calling a blocking database driver inside an
handler blocks the event loop, eliminating concurrency benefits. Requires care with thread pools for sync code.
FastAPI is designed for Pydantic. While you can use dataclasses or plain dicts, the ecosystem strongly assumes Pydantic models.
unlike Django, FastAPI ships with no database layer. You assemble SQLAlchemy, Tortoise, Beanie, or another library yourself.
Flask's extension ecosystem (Flask-Login, Flask-Mail, etc.) is larger and more mature. FastAPI is younger.
developers coming from synchronous Python need to understand
, event loops, and when to use
When a request arrives, uvicorn (the ASGI server) passes it to FastAPI's routing layer via the ASGI interface: a
coroutine to read the body, and a

coroutine to write the response. FastAPI's router matches the path against registered routes, instantiates dependency trees, validates inputs using Pydantic, awaits the handler coroutine, serialises the response through the response model, and calls

Pydantic validation happens at two points: on the way in (request body, path params, query params) and on the way out (response model). If input validation fails, FastAPI automatically returns a 422 Unprocessable Entity response with a detailed error list — no code needed in handlers.

Dependencies form a directed acyclic graph. When FastAPI invokes a handler, it first resolves the dependency graph depth-first, caching results for the lifetime of the request. A database session opened in a dependency is automatically closed (via the generator's finally block) after the handler returns.
wrapping an async generator. FastAPI calls
on the generator and writes each chunk to the ASGI send channel as it becomes available, enabling progressive delivery of LLM token streams or large file transfers.

The diagram below shows the full FastAPI request pipeline: the ASGI transport, middleware chain, dependency resolution, Pydantic validation at every boundary, handler execution on the asyncio event loop, and automatic OpenAPI generation.

attrs.alt: FastAPI request processing pipeline: HTTP → Starlette ASGI → middleware → dependencies → handler → Pydantic validation → JSON response, with auto-generated OpenAPI docs

content.text: FastAPI is the right choice for new Python REST API projects, especially when the service is IO-heavy (calling LLMs, reading from databases, making outbound HTTP calls). Its async model gives good performance without the complexity of manually managing thread pools.

Choose FastAPI when you want automatic validation and OpenAPI docs without boilerplate, when the team is comfortable with Python type annotations, and when you need to stream responses (LLM tokens, large file downloads, server-sent events).

Consider alternatives when: the project already has a large Flask or Django codebase (migration cost outweighs benefits); you need Django's admin interface or batteries-included auth; or you need the maximum possible raw throughput (gRPC or Rust-based frameworks outperform FastAPI for CPU-bound workloads).

pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard] pydantic

# Run development server with auto-reload
uvicorn main:app --reload --port 8000

Separate request models (what clients send) from response models (what the server returns). Response models let you control exactly which fields are exposed, preventing accidental exposure of internal state or secrets.
Step 3 — Define routes with type annotations
Path parameters become function arguments annotated with their type. FastAPI validates and parses them automatically — a 422 is returned if the type doesn't match.
to inject shared resources (DB sessions, current user, settings). Dependencies can be async generators that set up and tear down resources around the request lifecycle.
Middleware wraps every request/response. Add CORS, request logging, or request ID injection as middleware. Keep middleware lean — heavy logic belongs in dependencies or handlers.
Core patterns: routes, Pydantic, and dependency injection

from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional
from uuid import UUID, uuid4
from datetime import datetime

app = FastAPI(title="Agent API", version="1.0.0")

# ── Pydantic models ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class AgentCreate(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=100)
    model: str = Field(default="gpt-4o")
    system_prompt: Optional[str] = None

class AgentOut(BaseModel):
    id: UUID
    name: str
    model: str
    created_at: datetime

    model_config = {"from_attributes": True}

# ── Fake DB (replace with SQLAlchemy session in production) ─────────────────
_db: dict[str, dict] = {}

# ── Dependency: verified current agent ─────────────────────────────────────
async def get_agent_or_404(agent_id: UUID) -> dict:
    agent = _db.get(str(agent_id))
    if agent is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Agent not found")
    return agent

# ── Routes ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@app.post("/v1/agents", response_model=AgentOut, status_code=201)
async def create_agent(body: AgentCreate) -> AgentOut:
    agent = {
        "id": uuid4(),
        "name": body.name,
        "model": body.model,
        "created_at": datetime.utcnow(),
    }
    _db[str(agent["id"])] = agent
    return AgentOut(**agent)

@app.get("/v1/agents/{agent_id}", response_model=AgentOut)
async def get_agent(agent: dict = Depends(get_agent_or_404)) -> AgentOut:
    return AgentOut(**agent)

@app.get("/v1/agents", response_model=list[AgentOut])
async def list_agents(
    page: int = Query(1, ge=1),
    size: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=100),
) -> list[AgentOut]:
    all_agents = list(_db.values())
    start = (page - 1) * size
    return [AgentOut(**a) for a in all_agents[start:start + size]]

from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession, create_async_engine, async_sessionmaker
from fastapi import Depends
from typing import AsyncGenerator

DATABASE_URL = "postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@localhost/agents_db"

engine = create_async_engine(DATABASE_URL, pool_size=20, max_overflow=0)
AsyncSessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(engine, expire_on_commit=False)

async def get_db() -> AsyncGenerator[AsyncSession, None]:
    async with AsyncSessionLocal() as session:
        try:
            yield session
            await session.commit()
        except Exception:
            await session.rollback()
            raise

# Use in a route:
@app.get("/v1/agents/{agent_id}", response_model=AgentOut)
async def get_agent(
    agent_id: UUID,
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db),
):
    from sqlalchemy import select
    from app.models import Agent as AgentModel
    result = await db.execute(select(AgentModel).where(AgentModel.id == agent_id))
    agent = result.scalar_one_or_none()
    if agent is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Agent not found")
    return AgentOut.model_validate(agent)
Streaming LLM response with server-sent events

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import AsyncOpenAI
import asyncio, json

client = AsyncOpenAI()

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    messages: list[dict]
    model: str = "gpt-4o"

async def token_stream(request: ChatRequest):
    """Async generator that yields SSE-formatted chunks."""
    stream = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model=request.model,
        messages=request.messages,
        stream=True,
    )
    async for chunk in stream:
        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
        if delta:
            payload = json.dumps({"delta": delta})
            yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"
    yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"

@app.post("/v1/chat/stream")
async def stream_chat(body: ChatRequest):
    return StreamingResponse(
        token_stream(body),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={"X-Accel-Buffering": "no"},  # disable nginx buffering
    )

A production agent orchestration platform serves a FastAPI application that handles task submission, status polling, and result streaming for a multi-agent research pipeline. The platform processes 200–500 requests per second with 90th-percentile latency below 50 ms (excluding LLM call time).

from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException, BackgroundTasks
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.middleware.trustedhost import TrustedHostMiddleware
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
import uuid, asyncio

app = FastAPI(title="Agent Orchestration API", version="2.0.0")

# ── Security middleware ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
app.add_middleware(TrustedHostMiddleware, allowed_hosts=["api.example.com", "localhost"])
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["https://app.example.com"],
    allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "DELETE"],
    allow_headers=["Authorization", "Content-Type"],
)

# ── Schemas ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class TaskCreate(BaseModel):
    prompt: str
    agent_ids: list[str]
    max_steps: int = 10
    idempotency_key: Optional[str] = None  # prevent double-create

class TaskOut(BaseModel):
    id: str
    status: str
    result: Optional[str] = None

# ── Background runner ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def run_pipeline(task_id: str, task: TaskCreate):
    # Update status in Redis/DB, run agents, store result
    await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yield to event loop immediately

@app.post("/v1/tasks", response_model=TaskOut, status_code=202)
async def submit_task(
    body: TaskCreate,
    bg: BackgroundTasks,
    current_user=Depends(require_api_key),  # auth dependency
):
    task_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    bg.add_task(run_pipeline, task_id, body)
    return TaskOut(id=task_id, status="queued")

FastAPI running on uvicorn handles 10,000–30,000 requests per second on modern hardware for simple endpoints with no database IO. In practice the bottleneck is almost always the database or external service call. With asyncpg (async PostgreSQL) and connection pooling, single-instance throughput for DB-backed endpoints is typically 2,000–8,000 requests per second.
Scale by running multiple uvicorn workers (
) or by using gunicorn as a process manager (
). Each worker is a separate process with its own event loop and DB connection pool. Balance worker count against DB connection limits.
For CPU-bound handlers (embedding generation, tokenisation, image processing), use
). Never run CPU-bound work in an async handler without offloading — it starves the event loop.
Cache expensive dependency results. A FastAPI dependency decorated with
is computed once per process and reused. For per-request caching, store results in
for the high-performance uvloop and httptools event loop.
typed environment variable loading. Compatible with
async ORM with full support for asyncpg and aiosqlite.
pytest plugin for running async test functions. Pair with httpx
pre-built user authentication (registration, JWT, OAuth2) for FastAPI.
rate limiting middleware for FastAPI, based on limits.
structured logging that plays nicely with async code.

from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, Security
from fastapi.security import HTTPBearer, HTTPAuthorizationCredentials
from jose import jwt, JWTError

SECRET_KEY = "your-secret-key"  # use os.environ in production
ALGORITHM = "HS256"

bearer_scheme = HTTPBearer()

async def get_current_user(
    creds: HTTPAuthorizationCredentials = Security(bearer_scheme),
):
    token = creds.credentials
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=[ALGORITHM])
        user_id: str = payload.get("sub")
        if user_id is None:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid token")
    except JWTError:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid token")
    return user_id

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # startup: open DB pool, warm caches, etc.
    app.state.db_pool = await create_db_pool()
    yield
    # shutdown: close connections cleanly
    await app.state.db_pool.close()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

Think of FastAPI as a type-annotation-driven assembly line. You declare the shape of what comes in (Pydantic input model) and the shape of what goes out (Pydantic response model). FastAPI builds the machinery between them automatically: the parsing, the validation, the error responses, and the documentation. Your handler function is the only step that does custom work; everything around it is standardised.

The dependency injection system is like a catering service for your handlers. Before the handler runs, FastAPI resolves the dependency graph — opens a database connection, verifies the auth token, loads the current user — and places those resources on the handler's table. When the handler finishes, the cleanup callbacks run automatically. No manual resource management is needed in handler code.
FastAPI combines Starlette (ASGI transport) and Pydantic (validation) to deliver type-safe, async-native REST APIs with minimal boilerplate.
Type annotations are simultaneously the schema definition, the validator, and the OpenAPI documentation — write once, get three things for free.
Async handlers share one OS thread via the asyncio event loop; never call blocking code in an async handler — use asyncio.to_thread or an async library instead.
Dependency injection via Depends() handles shared resources (DB sessions, auth, settings) cleanly and makes handlers independently testable.
Use response_model to filter outgoing data — omitting it risks exposing internal fields like hashed passwords or internal IDs.
FastAPI scales horizontally: run multiple uvicorn workers behind a load balancer; the DB connection pool is the primary bottleneck to tune.
The /docs (Swagger UI) endpoint is generated automatically and usable as a live testing interface in development — no extra tooling required.
